video_title: How a Vibecoded Newsletter Is Making the Hay Market More Transparent | Odd Lots
gist: Aiden Johnson, the 20-year-old founder of Haywire, explains how he and a co-founder used AI/vibe coding to turn opaque USDA hay auction data plus phone-call 'ground intel' into a plain-English pricing newsletter. The conversation surfaces a structurally opaque, hyper-local, $8B alfalfa market under acute drought-driven supply stress, and the playbook for monetizing neglected public data.

insights.claim: The US hay market is not a single market but a collection of fragmented, hyper-local regional markets because high transport costs prevent moving hay across the country.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Pricing signals and trades must be evaluated region-by-region; there is no national benchmark to arbitrage against, so local supply/demand dominates.
insights.quote: it's not just one market. Um, it's like fragmented markets across the whole country. Every market, every regional market is very hyper local

insights.claim: Transporting hay typically costs $5 to $8 per mile via brokers and dealers, which can easily exceed the value of the hay itself over distance.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Transport economics cap regional price arbitrage and explain why coastal/eastern markets stay insulated from western shortages; a natural ceiling on price convergence.
insights.quote: these brokers, these dealers will charge anywhere from $5 to $8 per mile to have it shipped. So, that can easily average to be more valuable than the hay.

insights.claim: There is no agreed benchmark hay price; the USDA NASS publishes only average costs, and some Midwest participants use Rock Valley auction data as their de facto regional benchmark.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Absence of a reference price is the precondition that blocks a hedgeable, financialized hay market — the whitespace Haywire is trying to fill.
insights.quote: Rock Valley specifically is in the Midwest, um, use their auction data as the benchmark of kind of what hay is going for.

insights.claim: Deals in the hay market are often struck blindly by phone with no reference price, e.g. a buyer simply asking '$157 per ton' and the seller agreeing, leaving margins arbitrary.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Illustrates the raw informational inefficiency a transparency layer monetizes — and why incumbents resist it.
insights.quote: can I just do $157 um per ton for, you know, x amount of bales or tons? And he was like, yeah, yeah, that works. So, we're not going off anything here. There's no transparency.

insights.claim: Middlemen in commodity markets, including hay brokers, are the participants who lose most from greater price transparency because they profit from making deals blindly.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Identifies who will resist transparency tools and where margin compression lands as data products penetrate opaque commodity chains.
insights.quote: the middleman will always feel the u out or the outcomes of more transparency because they're the ones making deals u especially in the hay market kind of blindly and kind of just guessing

insights.claim: 46% of US alfalfa acreage is currently in drought, concentrating supply stress in western states.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: A hard supply-side stress metric supporting a bullish near-term price view for hay/alfalfa and watchpoint for downstream feed costs.
insights.quote: 46% of the US alphalfpha acreage is actually in a drought.

insights.claim: First-cut hay yields are coming in extremely light and 2025 carryover stocks are essentially gone, with buyers stockpiling ahead of summer.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Light first cut plus zero carryover signals tightening supply into summer — a setup for continued price spikes.
insights.quote: I'm hearing that first cut is being extremely light. the yields are not really producing at a volume and carryover from 2025 is essentially gone.

insights.claim: USDA average hay prices rose to roughly $180/ton in April (from $167 in March), with alfalfa around $185/ton (from $175 in March).
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Concrete month-over-month price levels confirming the upward trend; usable reference points for tracking the move.
insights.quote: right now, I'm looking at hay for April, it's $180 per ton, which is up $167 from March. And then for the alpha alpha side of things, it's $185 a ton, which is up from $175 since March.

insights.claim: Western drought may be pulling demand eastward, a pattern Haywire dubbed the 'Missouri pattern' after observing Missouri prices rise, then Rock Valley (Iowa) prices spike in subsequent weeks.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: A leading-indicator framework: watch western Midwest auction prices to anticipate demand migration and price contagion eastward.
insights.quote: we saw demand in or prices in Missouri go up... then the next week we saw um Rock Valley's prices kind of spike a little bit

insights.claim: A farmer pulled hay back from auction because tightening demand made him expect prices to rise further and he wanted to keep the supply, signaling hoarding behavior.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Withholding supply amid expected scarcity is a self-reinforcing dynamic that can accelerate price spikes.
insights.quote: he actually pulled it back because the demand for hay was getting so increasingly like it was getting really tight and he needed the hay. So, he's kind of predicting it to get even tighter.

insights.claim: Hay quality is graded by Relative Feed Value (RFV) on a base of 100, with roughly 150+ considered high quality; protein resides in the leaf and fiber in the stem, and rain during curing lowers protein and quality.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: RFV is the key quality metric segmenting the market; weather during harvest directly determines which buyer tier (dairy/equine vs beef) the hay serves.
insights.quote: RFV. a relative feed value... it's a base of 100. So anything like 150 I think is pretty good.

insights.claim: Demand is segmented by buyer: dairies and horse owners buy high-RFV alfalfa/supreme hay while beef cattle take lower-quality utility hay, and dairies blend high and low RFV to control rations and stretch volume amid high prices.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Price stress flows differently by segment; blending behavior by dairies is a demand-side shock absorber worth watching when premium prices spike.
insights.quote: these dairies are going to be the ones buying the higher quality RF alalfa to um you know, feed these cows that are producing 80 pounds of milk a day. And they're actually taking that high higher quality RF and then mixing it with lower quality RF

insights.claim: Higher hay prices are raising second-order costs such as horse boarding fees and pushing equine operations toward unprofitability, with a Texas horse sanctuary reportedly near closure due to hay and gas costs.
insights.speaker: Tracy Alloway / Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Demonstrates cost pass-through from feed commodities into adjacent service costs and potential demand destruction in the equine segment.
insights.quote: We love our horse so much, but the return on investment on horses right now is just like it's too it's too expensive.

insights.claim: Alfalfa covers part of roughly 49-50 million acres of US hay acreage and is an ~$8 billion-per-year crop, ranking as the fourth most valuable US crop.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Quantifies the market's scale — large enough to justify dedicated data products and a potential futures market despite its opacity.
insights.quote: you have hay acorage covering 49 to 50 million acres across the US and itself, alalfa itself is producing 8 billion dollars per year, which is the fourth most valuable crop.

themes.theme: Land use competition: data centers, solar, crops

insights.claim: Data centers built on agricultural land compete with alfalfa for acreage, and if sited in drought-prone western states their cooling could compete with water-intensive alfalfa for water.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: A non-obvious long-term supply constraint linking AI infrastructure buildout to feed-crop supply and water scarcity in the West.
insights.quote: alalfa is already a very water intrusive plant. So then alalfa is going to have to be competing with these big data centers for water.

insights.claim: Google is building a 482-acre data center near Jamestown/southern North Dakota that will be air-cooled, illustrating direct conversion of agricultural land to data infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Concrete local example of farmland-to-datacenter conversion pressuring crop acreage.
insights.quote: Google is actually going to build a 482 acre data center 20 minutes south of me

insights.claim: Landowners face four main options for idle acreage: rent to farmers per acre, enroll in USDA conservation (which locks up land access for 25-35 years), lease to solar developers for large payments, or grow the highest-value crop.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Maps the competing economic uses determining how much acreage stays in hay/crop production versus energy or conservation.
insights.quote: the USDA will basically pay you to conserve it and then the the downside of that is you probably lose your land or access to your land for 25 to 35 years.

insights.claim: Solar developers are paying farmland owners large sums to install panels, with whole farms in the Northeast already converted entirely to solar fields.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson / Tracy Alloway
insights.so_what: Solar leasing is an active competing land use removing acreage from agricultural production, tightening crop supply.
insights.quote: these solar panel companies are coming in to maybe place solar panel panels on your fields um for a huge chunk of change

insights.claim: A durable business can be built by finding free public data nobody regularly watches, aggregating it into a central repository, and becoming the recognized authority ('the axe') who comments on it.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal
insights.so_what: A repeatable playbook for monetizing neglected public datasets across many opaque markets — the core investable thesis of the episode.
insights.quote: find a piece of public data that exists that is out there that is consumable for free that nobody is regularly looking at gather it become the central repository for it and then occasionally comment on it

insights.claim: AI and vibe coding make it newly economical for two people to build a market-data company that would previously have required a large team, unlocking niche markets too small to serve before.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson / Joe Weisenthal
insights.so_what: The cost collapse in data tooling expands the addressable universe of viable micro-data businesses — a structural AI productivity unlock.
insights.quote: it makes it able for, you know, two young 20-year-olds like me and my co-founder Cole to, you know, mess around and create a whole company and platform.

insights.claim: Haywire combines a direct USDA API feed of weekly auction prices with phone-based 'ground intel' from auction houses, cross-referencing the two to validate data and filter out errors or 'hallucinations.'
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: The triangulation method — official data plus human calls — mirrors how illiquid benchmarks like LIBOR and scrap metal were historically priced.
insights.quote: we get live feed strictly from them and that basically funnels into our four system type of I guess you could say verification

insights.claim: Haywire monetizes via a freemium newsletter, with a pro tier at $14/month rising to $17/month after June 30, plus a potential API/data-licensing business line.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal / Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Low price point means scale or low headcount is essential; API licensing is the higher-margin path that could expand the business beyond newsletters.
insights.quote: It's just $14 a month... the price of being a founding member is going to rise to $17 a month after June 30th.

insights.claim: Older-generation farmers are reluctant to adopt technology, making distribution and onboarding the hardest part of building a transparency product in agriculture.
insights.speaker: Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Adoption friction, not data collection, is the key go-to-market risk for ag-data startups; networking and free data-sharing offers are the workaround.
insights.quote: especially these old gen farmers, they're not really going to adapt to technology like that. So it's been kind of uh difficult trying to get them to adapt

insights.claim: A tradable, hedgeable hay market cannot exist until an agreed reference index is established, and hay's extreme weather-driven volatility is precisely what makes a hedging instrument valuable yet hard to standardize.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal / Aiden Johnson
insights.so_what: Whoever establishes the credible hay benchmark could enable hay futures — a large optionality on top of the data business.
insights.quote: you can't have a hedgeable tradable financialized market until you first have that index, right? something that everyone agrees on as a reference price
